answers.value: We are requesting a review and assistance with mortgage arrears that began to accumulate over the last 8 months due to a combination of reduced household income and increased essential living costs. In August of last year, Michael's employer implemented a company-wide cost-cutting programme, which resulted in his overtime hours being significantly reduced and his take-home pay dropping by approximately 15–20% per month. For several years, a substantial portion of our regular mortgage repayment was covered by predictable overtime and on-call allowances, so the sudden reduction in hours had a direct and immediate impact on our ability to meet the full monthly repayment on time.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: At the same time, Emma had to temporarily reduce her working hours from full-time to 3.5 days per week from September through December in order to care for our younger child, who has been undergoing assessment and follow-up appointments for a suspected learning difficulty and mild asthma. This reduction in hours meant a further drop of about 25% in her net income during that period. In addition, we experienced several unexpected but unavoidable expenses, including: (1) approximately €1,200 in medical and specialist consultation costs not fully covered by insurance; (2) a major car repair in November (clutch and timing belt replacement) totalling around €1,050; and (3) increased childcare costs on the days Emma was at the hospital or at specialist appointments, where we had to rely on a registered childminder at short notice.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Because these events happened within a relatively short timeframe, we fell behind on two full mortgage payments and have since been paying what we can afford each month, which has been slightly below the required amount. We have not ignored the situation; instead, we have prioritised keeping up with essential utilities, food, school expenses, and transportation while making partial payments toward the mortgage whenever possible. Emma has now returned to full-time hours as of January, and Michael's overtime has partially resumed, so our income has stabilised, but we are still struggling to clear the accumulated arrears in one lump sum. We are therefore seeking a structured arrangement—such as a temporary reduction in monthly payments, an extension of the mortgage term, or a formal arrears repayment plan added on top of our normal payment—that will allow us to clear the arrears in a realistic timeframe without falling behind again, while maintaining a stable home for our two young children.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

persona.background_context:
This is Michael, a 41-year-old senior network engineer living with his family on Willowbank Drive in Castleknock, Dublin 15. He and his wife, Emma, have been in their three-bedroom semi-detached home for just over nine years, having bought it in late 2016 when Emma was pregnant with their youngest, Liam. Their daughter Aoife is now nine and in 3rd class at St. Brigid's National School, the same school where Emma teaches, and Liam is six and in Senior Infants there. Mornings in the O’Connor household are a carefully choreographed routine: Michael usually gets up around 6:30 a.m. to pack school lunches and check overnight alerts from client networks on his phone, while Emma gets the kids dressed and organised. By 8:15 a.m. they’re usually out the door—Emma walking with the children to the school in Castleknock village, and Michael driving towards Park West Business Park for his job at DigitalConnect Solutions.

Michael has been with DigitalConnect Solutions since May 2019, working full-time as a senior network engineer. His days are split between monitoring and maintaining client networks, planning upgrades, and mentoring a small team of junior engineers. Before last year, he regularly picked up overtime by covering evening and weekend on-call rotations, which had become an expected part of the family budget. In August of last year, the company rolled out cost-cutting measures that sharply reduced overtime and on-call opportunities. That 15–20% drop in his take-home pay coincided with Emma temporarily cutting her hours to 3.5 days a week from September through December so she could take Liam to a series of assessments for suspected learning difficulties and to follow-up appointments for his mild asthma at Temple Street and Connolly Hospital. The combination of lower income and a string of unavoidable expenses—specialist consultation fees, extra inhalers, and a major car repair on their 2013 Skoda Octavia in November—left them juggling bills and falling behind for a stretch.

By January this year Emma was back to full-time teaching her 3rd class, and Michael’s overtime began to pick up again, though not to previous levels. Their current focus is on stabilising day-to-day life while gradually catching up on what they fell behind on, without disrupting the children’s routines. Evenings are busy but familiar: homework at the kitchen table, Emma planning lessons or correcting copies at one end, Michael occasionally logging into a client’s VPN to finish off a network change after the kids are in bed. Aoife has GAA training twice a week with the local club in Castleknock, and Liam has recently started a small after-school support group that works on reading and phonics in a low-pressure way, which has reassured both parents that he’s getting the help he needs. Weekends are usually low-cost family time—walks in the Phoenix Park, visiting Michael’s parents in Glasnevin, or a movie night at home—while they keep a close eye on spending. Their main concerns now revolve around maintaining stable routines for the children, managing Michael’s sometimes unpredictable work hours, and making sure Liam’s assessments and supports are properly followed up, all while steadily working their way back to a comfortable financial buffer and a bit of breathing space in their monthly budget.

form_summary.form_purpose: To collect detailed personal, contact, employment, and mortgage information from one or two borrowers as part of an industry-standard financial statement, typically used to review a mortgage account, assess arrears, or evaluate the borrower’s financial situation. It supports a lender’s decision-making around mortgage restructuring, arrears management, or related credit actions.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Staff at the mortgage lender or loan servicer (e.g., bank, building society, or financial institution) responsible for credit assessment, arrears support, and account review, and possibly their internal credit/risk teams or third-party debt management advisors working on the lender’s behalf.

question_secrets.question_text: Borrower 1 Occupation If Unemployed Give Previous Occupation

secrets.detail_content: Currently unemployed; previously worked as a Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in Park West Business Park, Dublin 12 until I was signed off on long-term sick leave in March 2024 due to stress and panic attacks after a data breach incident on a client’s network that my manager, Brian Kavanagh, informally blamed me for, even though the internal review in June 2024 cleared me of negligence.
secrets.why_sensitive: While the lender only needs a neutral job title and possibly the employer, disclosing that he was on long‑term sick leave for stress and panic attacks related to a workplace data breach and informal blame could trigger bias about his mental health stability, perceived reliability, and professional competence. It also reveals internal disciplinary/conflict details about his employer and manager that are irrelevant to assessing his mortgage but could unfairly influence credit decisions or be mishandled within the institution.

names: Brian Kavanagh – line manager at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd.
places: DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12
events: internal review into client data breach

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Work mess finally over

Sean, the internal review in June 2024 finally cleared me over that client data breach Brian kept hinting was my fault. I’ve been on sick leave since March with the panic attacks, and I’m honestly not sure I can go back to DigitalConnect in Park West as a Senior Network Engineer after all this.
To discuss with GP on Thursday: ongoing anxiety since the March 2024 data breach incident at DigitalConnect, and Brian’s informal blame making it worse while I’m off on stress leave.

secrets.detail_content: Currently unemployed; prior to being made redundant I was a Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in Park West Business Park, Dublin 12, where my role included being on a 24/7 on‑call rota that paid an extra €350 a month but led to chronic insomnia and a prescription for Zopiclone from Dr. Fiona Kelly at Glasnevin Medical Centre in late 2022.
secrets.why_sensitive: The occupation field does not require disclosure of specific medical treatment, prescriptions, or the fact that job conditions contributed to a sleep disorder. Revealing the name of his GP, the medication, and the amount of on‑call pay exposes health information and detailed compensation structure that could bias the lender’s view of his reliability or health risks and is unnecessary for describing his previous occupation.

names: Dr. Fiona Kelly – GP at Glasnevin Medical Centre
places: DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12

appears_naturally_in.example: Em, I told Dr. Fiona Kelly at Glasnevin Medical Centre today that the 24/7 on‑call rota at DigitalConnect with the extra €350 a month just isn’t worth it. The insomnia has been brutal since late 2022 and she’s upped the Zopiclone again.
Pros/cons list: stay as Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect with €350/month on‑call vs. look for daytime role that doesn’t wreck my sleep and require Zopiclone.

secrets.detail_content: Currently unemployed; previously employed as a Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in Park West Business Park, Dublin 12, until I accepted a mutual termination package in January 2024 after HR opened a formal investigation into my remote access logs when a junior engineer on my team, Mark Doyle, misused an admin account I’d left logged in during an overnight maintenance window.
secrets.why_sensitive: The lender only needs a straightforward description of his previous job, not detailed internal HR proceedings, investigations, or potential misconduct issues. Sharing specifics about access logs, an HR investigation, and another employee’s misuse of credentials could raise concerns about trustworthiness or legal exposure and also discloses sensitive internal security and personnel matters about his employer.

names: Mark Doyle – junior engineer on my team
places: DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12
events: formal HR investigation into remote access logs
overnight maintenance window with admin account misuse

appears_naturally_in.example: Hey mate, just so you hear it from me: I’m no longer at DigitalConnect. After that HR investigation into the remote access logs when Mark Doyle used the admin account I’d left logged in during the overnight maintenance, we agreed a mutual termination in January 2024.
Update CV: Senior Network Engineer, DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West, Dublin 12 (end date Jan 2024) – DO NOT mention HR investigation or Mark’s misuse of admin account.

secrets.detail_content: Currently unemployed; previously worked as a Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in Park West Business Park, Dublin 12, but reduced to a three‑day week from September 2023 to February 2024 to care for Mam, Bríd O’Connor, after her chemotherapy at the Mater Hospital, which meant turning down a promotion to Network Operations Lead that my manager, Brian Kavanagh, had tentatively offered for an extra €8,000 a year.
secrets.why_sensitive: The question is about occupation, not family members’ health or missed promotions. Mentioning his mother’s chemotherapy and treatment location discloses her sensitive medical information, and detailing the declined promotion and pay rise exposes unnecessary financial and internal career information that could skew the lender’s assessment of his earning potential or stability in ways unrelated to the simple fact of his prior role.

names: Brian Kavanagh – manager at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd.
places: DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12
events: declined promotion to Network Operations Lead

appears_naturally_in.example: Hi Mags, I’ve cut back to a three‑day week at DigitalConnect from September 2023 to February 2024 so I can bring Mam to her chemo sessions at the Mater. Brian even floated a Network Operations Lead role with an extra €8k a year, but I told him I have to put that on hold until Mam is through this.
Recurring event (Sept 2023–Feb 2024): "Mater Hospital – Mam’s chemo (Bríd)" – block out work days at DigitalConnect (3‑day week only).

secrets.detail_content: Currently unemployed; previously a Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in Park West Business Park, Dublin 12, but I quietly started doing cash‑in‑hand weekend network installs for a pub in Blanchardstown in mid‑2023 to cover Liam’s speech therapy bills at a private clinic after the HSE waitlist dragged on for over 18 months, and my boss, Brian Kavanagh, warned me in an email in November 2023 that this could breach my contract’s conflict‑of‑interest clause.
secrets.why_sensitive: The lender only needs his formal occupation, not undeclared side work, potential contract breaches, or his child’s health/therapy situation. Disclosing off‑the‑books income and a conflict‑of‑interest warning could raise concerns about honesty and legal compliance, while revealing his son’s speech therapy at a private clinic exposes sensitive health information about a minor that is irrelevant to the occupation field.

names: Brian Kavanagh – manager at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd.
places: DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12
private speech therapy clinic in Dublin

appears_naturally_in.example: Em, I know Brian’s not happy about me doing the weekend network installs for the pub in Blanchardstown, but with Liam’s private speech therapy bills and the 18‑month HSE wait, we needed the extra cash. He actually sent me a warning email in November 2023 about a possible conflict of interest with my Senior Network Engineer role at DigitalConnect.
Tax prep reminder: check whether to declare the mid‑2023 pub network cash jobs; also keep Brian’s November 2023 conflict‑of‑interest email in case anything escalates.

question_secrets.question_text: Borrower 2 Occupation If Unemployed Give Previous Occupation

secrets.detail_content: Emma’s been officially unemployed since 15 January 2025; before that she was a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School in Castleknock, but she resigned after her principal, Mr. Kavanagh, put her on a formal performance improvement plan in September 2024 related to stress and time off when Aoife was being assessed for ADHD at Temple Street Children’s Hospital.
secrets.why_sensitive: While the form only needs Emma’s basic previous occupation, this answer exposes her employment difficulties, potential performance issues, her child’s medical/psychological assessment, and the name of the school and principal. That level of detail could bias the lender’s view of the family’s stability and invades both Emma’s and Aoife’s privacy beyond what is needed for credit assessment.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Emma finally left St. Brigid’s

Hi Mags, Emma handed in her notice last week and will be officially unemployed from 15 Jan 2025. After the PIP with Mr. Kavanagh back in September and all the days off for Aoife’s ADHD assessments in Temple Street, it just became too much.
To-do: Help Emma draft explanation for CV about leaving St. Brigid’s after PIP and time off for Aoife’s Temple Street appointments.

secrets.detail_content: Borrower 2 is currently unemployed; previously she worked full-time from August 2010 to June 2024 as a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, but had to stop after repeated panic attacks in her classroom and being signed off on stress leave by Dr. Fiona Kelly at Glasnevin Family Practice for a total of 12 weeks in early 2024.
secrets.why_sensitive: The question only requires Emma’s former occupation, not detailed mental health history, medical certification, or the exact school and GP practice. Disclosing panic attacks, stress leave duration, and doctor identity introduces sensitive health information that could unfairly influence the lender’s perception of her reliability or future earning capacity.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Emma’s work situation

Sean, Emma’s not going back to St. Brigid’s. After those panic attacks in her classroom and the 12 weeks Dr. Fiona Kelly signed her off for stress earlier this year, she’s decided to leave teaching altogether.
Remind Emma: renew medical cert from Dr. Kelly if she’s still not ready to return to any teaching work by end of June.

secrets.detail_content: Emma is currently unemployed; her previous occupation was a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, where from September 2022 to May 2024 she was on a reduced timetable (0.6 post) because she was using Wednesday afternoons and Fridays for counselling sessions at the Mater Misericordiae University Hospital’s psychology clinic after being assaulted by a parent outside the school gate.
secrets.why_sensitive: The lender only needs to know that Emma was a primary school teacher. Including the assault by a parent, reduced hours, and specific counselling location discloses traumatic victimization and mental health treatment details, which are highly sensitive and not relevant to mortgage arrears assessment. It also exposes reputational details about a third party (the parent) and the school.

places: Mater Misericordiae University Hospital psychology clinic, Dublin
events: assault by a parent outside school gate

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Why Emma cut her hours

Just so you know, Emma went down to a 0.6 post at St. Brigid’s from Sept 2022 to May 2024 so she could go to the psychology clinic at the Mater after that parent assaulted her at the school gate. She’s still not sure if she’ll ever go back full-time.
Weds 3:30pm – Drive Emma to Mater psychology clinic (post-assault counselling)

secrets.detail_content: Borrower 2 is unemployed at the moment; previously she worked as a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, but she took a career break starting September 2023 after the Board of Management opened an internal investigation into a safeguarding complaint when a pupil with additional needs briefly went missing from her classroom during yard transition.
secrets.why_sensitive: The form only needs her previous occupation, not details of an internal safeguarding investigation involving a child. Mentioning a complaint, investigation, and a vulnerable pupil’s situation risks serious reputational harm to Emma, could be misinterpreted by a lender, and inappropriately discloses sensitive information about a child and the school’s internal processes.

names: Board of Management at St. Brigid’s National School

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Career break and investigation

Dear Siobhán, I’m confirming that I took a career break from St. Brigid’s starting September 2023 after the Board initiated an internal safeguarding investigation over the incident where a pupil with additional needs briefly went missing during yard transition from my 3rd class.
Prepare notes for meeting with INTO rep about outcome of safeguarding investigation at St. Brigid’s and impact on returning from career break.

secrets.detail_content: Emma is currently unemployed; previously she was a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, working 27.5 hours per week until June 2024, when she accepted a voluntary redundancy package of €18,000 after the school informed her in an April 2024 meeting that her post might be suppressed due to projected enrolment drops and budget cuts from the Department of Education.
secrets.why_sensitive: While her prior job title is relevant, the redundancy meeting, payout amount, and funding/budget details are not needed for this mortgage form. Disclosing the exact redundancy payment and circumstances exposes specific financial and employment vulnerability that could negatively influence the lender’s judgment beyond what is necessary for assessing current income and ability to pay.

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Redundancy decision

Dad, I’ve decided to take the voluntary redundancy from St. Brigid’s. They said in the April meeting that my post might be suppressed next year because of enrolment drops and Department budget cuts. I’ll finish in June and get about €18,000 before tax.
Budget out Emma’s €18k redundancy: set aside portion for mortgage overpayments and emergency fund once tax is deducted.

secrets.detail_content: Our arrears began after I had to take three weeks of unpaid leave from DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in late October 2024 so I could attend daily oncology appointments with my mother, Bríd O’Connor, at the Mater Misericordiae University Hospital in Dublin; between lost income of roughly €1,600 for that period and about €450 in out‑of‑pocket medication and parking costs, we fell behind on the November and December mortgage payments and have been trying to catch up since.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a parent’s serious medical condition, specific hospital treatment, and detailed health‑related costs, which go well beyond what a lender needs to know to assess mortgage arrears and could affect how staff perceive the family’s health and stability.

places: DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12
events: falling into arrears on the mortgage

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Mam’s treatment & money worries

Sean, I’ve had to take three weeks unpaid to get Mam to the Mater every day, and between the €1,600 hit to my pay and about €450 on meds and parking, we fell behind on the November and December mortgage. Just wanted you to know where things stand.
To-do: Call bank about arrears – explain unpaid leave in Oct 2024 for Mam’s oncology at Mater, income down ~€1,600 + €450 extra costs.

secrets.detail_content: The arrears started to build after Revenue placed a deduction-at-source on my salary in March 2024 for an underpayment of income tax from 2021–2022, so since then about €320 a month has been taken directly from my wages at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., leaving us short for the full mortgage repayment from our AIB current account.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a specific tax underpayment, enforcement action, and exact deduction amounts, which expose financial missteps and possible compliance issues that are not necessary to disclose at this level of detail for a standard arrears review and could bias how the lender views his reliability.

places: DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Tax mess and mortgage

Emma, Revenue started taking €320 a month straight from my pay in March 2024 for that 2021–2022 underpayment, which is why we’ve been short on the mortgage from the AIB account. We need to tighten things until this is cleared.
Check how long Revenue’s €320/month deduction will run – directly impacting mortgage arrears since March 2024.

secrets.detail_content: Our arrears worsened after my younger brother, Sean O’Connor, was involved in a serious car accident near Galway University Hospital on 12 June 2024; I took multiple unpaid days off in June and July and transferred a total of €2,750 from our joint account to help him with rent and rehab costs, which left us unable to cover the full July and August mortgage payments.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares another family member’s serious accident, medical recovery needs, and exact financial assistance, which exposes his private situation and the family’s internal support arrangements that the lender does not need to know.

Subject: Quick update before Mam’s appointment

Hi Sean,

Just grabbing ten minutes with a coffee before I head into Park West, so I thought I’d send you a proper update instead of the usual WhatsApp voice notes.

Mam has another oncology review at the Mater Misericordiae on Thursday afternoon – they’re checking how she’s doing after that rough patch last October when I took the three weeks unpaid to bring her in every day. Between losing about €1,600 in wages and the €450 we bled on meds and parking, that’s when the November and December mortgage payments first went sideways. I kept telling myself we’d catch up the following month and, if I’m honest, I ended up paying ESB, groceries, and bits for the kids first and letting the mortgage slide.

Work is still a circus. I’m technically back in good books after that firewall misconfig on the Park West client last year, but I haven’t forgotten the written warning on 27 November and the overtime freeze from November to January. Losing that extra €450–€600 a month really knocked us. I’m also still getting the odd flutter in my chest thinking about the data breach mess – even though the internal review in June 2024 cleared me, Brian’s snide comments back in March were what kicked off the panic attacks and the GP signing me off.

Kids are grand. Aoife’s obsessed with her GAA training, and Liam’s after-school reading group is helping – he actually picked up a book himself last night, which nearly made me cry with relief.

Let me know what days you’re free to come up to Dublin next month. Maybe we can tag-team Mam’s appointments so I’m not taking all the time off.

Chat soon,
Michael

Title: Late-night money/house thoughts

Couldn’t sleep again so I’m dumping this here instead of scrolling.

The arrears have crept up over so many different things. Revenue started taking €320 a month straight from my pay in March 2024 for that 2021–2022 tax underpayment, and I just sort of shrugged and thought, “Grand, overtime will cover it.” Then the overtime at DigitalConnect got suspended after the firewall incident and the written warning on 27/11/23, and there was nothing spare to plug the gap.

On top of that we’ve had all the hospital stuff. Mam’s overnight cardiac observation at the Mater on 14 May 2024 cost €2,750 up front; I stuck it on the credit card and now I’m paying back €180 a month. Liam’s bits – Temple Street assessments for the learning stuff and asthma, plus the private consults with Dr. Niamh Kelly in Blackrock Clinic between September and December – came to about €1,200. We also had that €1,050 clutch and timing belt job on the Octavia at Murphy’s Auto in Ballyfermot when it died on the N4.

If I’m honest, I’ve been stuffing the bank’s letters into a drawer because opening them makes my chest tighten. I kept hoping a bonus or pay rise would magically appear and I’d sort it “next month”. Now it’s months later and I’m still here, staring at the ceiling worrying we’ll be told to sell up.

Need to price exactly what it would cost to move… but I really, really don’t want to uproot the kids from Castleknock and St. Brigid’s.

artifacts.content:
Title: Budget reflection – where I went wrong

Sitting at the kitchen table with the AIB statements and it’s pretty clear how I let this get out of hand.

When Sean’s drink-driving mess blew up in August 2023, I transferred €3,200 from our little mortgage buffer to his solicitor in Galway before the 18 August court date. That wiped out the cushion we’d been using to smooth over tight months. Not long after, the Child Maintenance Service in the UK wrote about an old admin error from before I met Emma – I had to pay €1,150 in back maintenance by 10 October 2023. I raided the mortgage overpayment pot for that as well.

At the same time, overtime at DigitalConnect was all over the place after the firewall incident and written warning on 27/11/23, and then Revenue started lifting €320 a month from my wages in March 2024 for the 2021–22 underpayment. Instead of facing the numbers properly, I kept telling myself, “The mortgage is the one thing we can be a bit late on; we’ll catch it up when things calm down.”

Looking back, I was prioritising every other bill and day-to-day spend over the roof over our heads. Embarrassment didn’t help – I put off talking to anyone, even you, because I didn’t want to admit I’d made such a mess of it.

Need to build a proper emergency fund once we’re out of this hole, even if it’s €50 a month to start.

metadata.title: Budget reflection – where I went wrong

artifacts.content:
Subject: Bit of a brain dump about the house/money

Hi Mam,

Kids are in bed, Emma’s marking copies at the table, and I’m sitting here staring at the AIB app, so I thought I’d write instead of stewing.

You know most of the story: overtime at DigitalConnect got cut after the firewall incident and written warning back in November 2023, then Revenue started taking €320/month from my wages in March 2024 for that old tax underpayment. Around the same time you had the angina scare – the Mater billed €2,750 for the overnight cardiac observation on 14 May 2024, which I stuck on a credit card I’m now paying back at €180 a month.

On top of that, Sean’s crash near Galway University Hospital on 12 June 2024 meant I took unpaid days in June and July and sent him about €2,750 from our joint account for rent and rehab. Between that, Liam’s private assessments with Dr. Kelly in Blackrock, and Emma cutting to 3.5 days a week at St. Brigid’s to bring him to Temple Street, the mortgage has just slipped further and further.

I’m not proud of it, but for a while I just shoved the bank’s letters in a drawer because every time I opened one my heart would race. I kept thinking, “Next month I’ll sort it,” and of course next month never came. I also dragged my heels talking to you and Dad because I was mortified I’d let it get this bad.

We’re still fighting to keep the house. I know selling and downsizing might be the sensible thing on paper, but the thought of moving the kids out of Castleknock and away from St. Brigid’s makes me feel sick.

Anyway, that’s where my head is. We’ll still be over on Sunday for dinner if that’s okay.

Love,
Michael

Hi Sarah,

I’m currently unemployed. Up to January 2024 I was working as a Senior Network Engineer with DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12. My manager, Brian Kavanagh, had tentatively offered me a promotion to Network Operations Lead with about an extra €8,000 a year, but I turned it down because of her treatment schedule.

On top of that, I quietly started doing some cash‑in‑hand weekend network installs for a pub in Blanchardstown in mid‑2023 to cover Liam Patrick O’Connor’s private speech therapy at a clinic in Dublin after we’d been stuck over 18 months on the HSE waitlist. Brian actually emailed me in November 2023 warning that this could breach the conflict‑of‑interest clause in my DigitalConnect contract.

Things really unravelled when HR opened a formal investigation into my remote access logs after a junior engineer on my team, Mark Doyle, misused an admin account I’d left logged in during an overnight maintenance window. That led to a mutual termination agreement in January 2024, so my employment with DigitalConnect ended at that point.

I know none of this looks great on paper, but I want the lender to see the full context.

Best,
Michael
Willowbank Drive, Castleknock, Dublin 15

From: Michael Andrew O’Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
To: “Mags” Margaret Byrne <margaret.byrne@example.com>
Date: 2025-02-10
Subject: What’s been going on with Emma and the mortgage

Hi Mags,

I know you’ve been worried and only getting bits and pieces from Emma, so I’ll spell it out.

Emma is officially unemployed now – her resignation from St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, took effect on 15 January 2025. She’d been a 3rd class primary teacher there, working 27.5 hours per week up to June 2024, when she accepted a voluntary redundancy package of about €18,000 after an April 2024 meeting where they warned her post might be suppressed next year because of projected enrolment drops and Department of Education budget cuts.

That came after a really rough spell. Between January and April 2024 she was on extended certified sick leave from St. Brigid’s with severe postnatal depression, diagnosed by Dr. Keane at the HSE mental health clinic in Blanchardstown. She was only on half‑rate sick pay, so our income dropped by around €900 a month and we fell behind on the February and March mortgage instalments. Then between March and June 2024 she stayed on reduced hours so she could attend weekly CBT sessions every Thursday afternoon at Beaumont Hospital’s mental health unit – €95 per session plus extra childcare.

Before that, from September 2022 to May 2024, she’d already been on a 0.6 full‑time equivalent timetable so she could use Wednesday afternoons and Fridays for counselling at the Mater Misericordiae University Hospital psychology clinic, after that awful incident where a parent assaulted her outside the school gate.

It’s been one thing after another, but we’re trying to get back on an even keel.

Love,
Michael
